elements.sent-2411-001: Accurate detection of locomotion transitions, such as walk to sit, walk to stair ascent, and descent, is crucial to effectively control robotic assistive devices, such as lower-limb exoskeletons, as each locomotion mode requires specific assistance.
elements.sent-2411-002: Variability in collected sensor data introduced by user- or system-specific characteristics makes it challenging to maintain high transition detection accuracy while avoiding latency using non-adaptive classification models.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this study, we identified key factors influencing transition detection performance, including variations in user behavior, and different mechanical designs of the exoskeletons.
elements.sent-2411-004: To boost the transition detection accuracy, we introduced two methods for adapting a finite-state machine classifier to system- and user-specific variability: a Statistics-Based approach and Bayesian Optimization.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our experimental results demonstrate that both methods remarkably improve transition detection accuracy across diverse users, achieving up to an 80\% increase in certain scenarios compared to the non-personalized threshold method.
elements.sent-2411-006: These findings emphasize the importance of personalization in adaptive control systems, underscoring the potential for enhanced user experience and effectiveness in assistive devices.
elements.sent-2411-007: By incorporating subject- and system-specific data into the model training process, our approach offers a precise and reliable solution for detecting locomotion transitions, catering to individual user needs, and ultimately improving the performance of assistive devices.
elements.sent-2411-008: Delivering effective assistance during various locomotion activities, such as walking, stair ascent, stair descent, and sitting, is critical for enhancing user experience in lower-limb exoskeletons.
elements.sent-2411-009: Accurate real-time identification of the transitions between different locomotion activities is crucial for developing intelligent systems that deliver seamless and responsive assistance.
elements.sent-2411-010: A key challenge in real-world exoskeleton applications lies in ensuring adaptability across diverse locomotion tasks.
elements.sent-2411-011: Most control strategies are designed for specific, steady-state activities, like level-ground walking, yet real-world environments demand systems that can handle varied terrains and frequent transitions with dynamic adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-012: Achieving precise transition detection across different users and conditions is particularly challenging.
elements.sent-2411-013: Variations in user gait, exoskeleton designs, and the need for efficient on-board processing further complicate reliable transition detection between locomotion modes.
elements.sent-2411-014: Most existing methods, often designed for specific locomotion tasks, can fall short when faced with more dynamic, real-world scenarios involving varied terrains and diverse user behaviors.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{OutdoorEnvironment_LQ.png} \caption{The outdoor environment where the onboard transition detection tests are performed for walk to stair ascend, stair ascend to walk, walk to sit, sit to walk, walk to stair descend, and stair descend to walk transitions} \label{fig:OnlineTestsOutdoorEnvironment}
elements.sent-2411-015: The finite-state machine (FSM) developed by Cheng et al. \cite{Cheng2021 offers a structured approach to transition detection, combining different locomotion modes into a single system that leverages key features like hip flexion/extension angle and velocity.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In our previous work, we extended this FSM to improve real-time performance on embedded systems, reducing processing time up to elevenfold with a machine learning-trained threshold method compared to deploying machine learning models directly \cite{Orhan2023_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: This FSM configuration achieved high accuracy with ten healthy subjects across four key locomotion modes: walking, sitting, stair ascent, and stair descent in an outdoor setting presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:OnlineTestsOutdoorEnvironment .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Testing this method on a different exoskeleton, autonomyo, revealed certain limitations.
elements.sent-2411-019: Unlike eWalk, which features only hip flexion/extension as the active degree of freedom per leg, autonomyo offers three active degrees of freedom, including hip abduction/adduction and knee flexion/extension, making it inherently more complex and heavier.
elements.sent-2411-020: During experiments, eWalk operated in passive mode, benefiting from its lightweight, transparent design, while autonomyo was used in zero-torque mode to maintain transparency.
elements.sent-2411-021: These design differences affected user behavior; with autonomyo, users exhibited slower sitting patterns, smaller pre-sit adjustment steps, and overall more cautious movement.
elements.sent-2411-022: Moreover, the FSM did not fully accommodate different styles of locomotion due to subject-specific characteristics, such as running-like stair climbing.
elements.sent-2411-023: These findings indicate that while detecting transitions is essential, personalizing this detection to accommodate individual differences can further enhance the efficacy of assistive devices.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this study we focus on enhancing transition detection algorithms by tailoring them to individual gait patterns and movement characteristics across two distinct exoskeletons.
elements.sent-2411-025: Leveraging real-time experimental data, we introduce two methods, a statistics-based approach and Bayesian optimization to personalize the FSM thresholds, improving detection accuracy by accounting for system- and user-specific variability.
elements.sent-2411-026: Our results demonstrate that these personalized adjustments significantly enhance adaptability and effectiveness, underscoring the potential for responsive, user-centered control in lower-limb exoskeletons and similar assistive devices.

elements.sent-2411-032: Locomotion mode identification involves recognizing various activities or movement patterns, such as walking, running, stair ascent, stair descent, and sitting \cite{Kolaghassi2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The goal is to accurately and rapidly detect the intended locomotion modes of assistive device users by analyzing real-time input signals, including kinematic, dynamic, and neuromuscular data.
elements.sent-2411-034: Commonly estimated locomotion modes include level ground walking, sit-to-stand transitions, stair ascent, and descent, and ramp ascent and descent \cite{Gehlhar2023, Kolaghassi2021, Wang2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Wearable sensors such as inertial measurement units (IMUs), force sensors, and electromyographic sensors, are widely used for locomotion mode detection \cite{Gehlhar2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: These studies often use feature extraction techniques to identify the unique characteristics of different activities, followed by classification algorithms to detect the activities based on the extracted features \cite{Allahbakhshi2019, Aldabbagh2020, Zhang2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The complexity of these different classification algorithms varies from simple threshold-based methods to more advanced algorithms such as deep learning approaches \cite{Preece2009, Wang2023, Pesenti2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Reported accuracies vary across different studies and depend on factors such as choice of sensors, feature extraction methods, and classification algorithms \cite{Attal2015, Pohl2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Most of the time the performance of the selected techniques is analyzed based on accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
elements.sent-2411-040: However, for onboard real-time implementation of suggested strategies, the low computational cost is also important \cite{Yuan2015, Narayan2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Since the performance of an assistive device is strongly affected by the response time or delay, an accurate but also fast transition detection strategy is essential \cite{LoboPrat2014, Aldabbagh2020, Kang2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: While the existing classification methods excel in locomotion type identification, many of these methods focus on steady-state activity detection for several locomotion modes \cite{Donahue2022, Gehlhar2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: However, detecting the transitions between different activities is crucial from the perspective of control of a lower-limb exoskeleton to be able to assist promptly with changes in locomotion, ensuring smooth human-robot interaction and enhancing the safety and efficiency of lower-limb exoskeletons \cite{Cheng2021, Qian2022, Zhang2024, Haque2024, Wang2024, Cheng_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: This necessitates the development of specialized algorithms that focus on transition detection rather than continuous activity recognition.
elements.sent-2411-045: Most studies rely heavily on sensors placed on the human body and often lack real-time experimental results integrated into lower-limb assistive devices \cite{Zhou2019, Lu2020_2, Narayan2022, Vu2022, Eken2023, Wang2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Few studies have conducted experiments with exoskeletons, with many focusing on offline recognition and analysis, and even fewer exploring online recognition in real-time conditions \cite{Labarriere2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In the field of lower-limb prosthesis, multiple methodologies are developed to achieve high real-time performance in recognizing different locomotion types for intuitive prosthetic control \cite{Young2014b, Su2019, Haque2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: However, the identification of transitions between locomotion modes with lower-limb exoskeletons has not been studied as comprehensively as in prosthetic systems.
elements.sent-2411-049: A review article \cite{Labarriere2020 highlights that only three studies investigated the transitions between locomotion modes among the eight studies focused on orthoses or exoskeletons.}
elements.sent-2411-050: A more recent review \cite{Moreira2022 , indicates an increasing number of studies aimed at detecting transitions between locomotion modes.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Long et al. \cite{Long2016 optimized support vector machine (SVM) with particle swarm techniques for a knee exoskeleton to identify five modes—level walking, stair and ramp ascent/descent, and transitions—using ground reaction forces and orientation data.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Tested with three subjects, the approach achieved low error rates but depended on future steps, limiting real-time applicability.
elements.sent-2411-053: Wang et al. \cite{Wang2018 used a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network on a soft knee exoskeleton to detect level walking, stair ascent/descent, and transitions with 98.2\% steady-state accuracy, though with a recognition delay of nearly one step.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Zhou et al. \cite{Zhou2019 employed SVM with IMU data for similar modes on a unilateral knee exoskeleton, while Zhang et al. \cite{Zhang2024 used random forests on a soft hip exosuit to detect level walking and stair ascent/descent with over 90\% accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: However, these approaches rely on fixed-length sliding windows, potentially limiting adaptability to variable walking speeds.
elements.sent-2411-056: Liu and Wang, \cite{Liu2020 implemented SVM to recognize the locomotion mode for a unilateral knee exoskeleton together with recognition of sitting.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Their experimental results demonstrated that the real-time recognition with two IMUs obtained satisfactory recognition accuracy and low delay time.
elements.sent-2411-058: A fuzzy logic-based algorithm for locomotion and transition mode recognition using inertial sensors mounted on a hip joint exoskeleton is proposed by Du et al. \cite{Du2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: This approach overcomes subject-dependent parameters in data training, avoiding a training procedure per subject.
elements.sent-2411-060: In contrast, subject-independent locomotion mode classification for a hip exoskeleton is suggested by \cite{Kang2022 based on a deep convolutional neural network.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Although this suggested strategy seems to be promising without fine-tuning subject-specific parameters, it has only been validated in offline scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-062: Most of the traditional machine learning (ML) approaches are efficient at handling complex patterns, generalizing them effectively, and offering versatility through various algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-063: However, they often require extensive feature engineering and are sensitive to feature quality \cite{Alzubaidi2023 , which can limit scalability.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In contrast, deep learning methods can automatically learn features from raw data, excel at capturing complex patterns, and scale well with the availability of large datasets.
elements.sent-2411-065: Despite these benefits, deep learning models demand significant amounts of labeled data, are computationally intensive, and often lack interpretability \cite{Alzubaidi2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Given the constraints of data availability and limited computational resources in assistive devices, this study utilizes an ML-trained threshold-based (TH) method.
elements.sent-2411-067: Unlike manual threshold setting, we employ traditional machine learning approaches to enhance the generalizability of our method, as suggested by Cheng et al. \cite{Cheng2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: In our previous study \cite{Orhan2023_2 , the ML-trained TH-method effectively captured the dynamics of transitions based on human subject experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-069: However, it did not detect all transitions in every scenario, as subjects exhibited varying dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-070: While the method for detecting transitions between locomotion modes was generally effective across a diverse user base, we identified the need for subject and/or system-specific optimization to accommodate individual differences, especially when exoskeletons impose greater kinematic constraints on user movements.
elements.sent-2411-071: It has been demonstrated that inter-subject variability in response to any given assistive strategy suggests that device parameters beneficial to one subject may hinder another \cite{Quesada2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-072: This leads to the hypothesis that overall performance across subjects could be improved by tuning control parameters for each individual \cite{Kim2017_2, Slade2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-073: The dual objectives of personalization and generalization present a fundamental challenge in the development of adaptive control systems for assistive devices.
elements.sent-2411-074: In the literature on lower-limb exoskeletons, initial approaches to personalization have relied on subject-specific training data \cite{Kim2017_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-075: However, effective personalization is often constrained by the impracticality of lengthy evaluation periods \cite{Zhang2017, Ding2018, Xu2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: To overcome this challenge, Bayesian optimization, an efficient global optimization strategy, is adopted for its data efficiency and noise tolerance \cite{Brochu2010, Jasper2012, Slade2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Bayesian optimization is utilized in a human-in-the-loop (HIL) strategy to reduce the user’s walking energy consumption using a wearable robot \cite{Ding2018, Kim2017_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: The HIL Bayesian optimization has been developed to minimize overall experiment time \cite{Wen2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: This minimal experimental time is crucial when prolonged physical exertion is challenging, especially for individuals with impaired mobility.
elements.sent-2411-080: Building on prior research, our work leverages data from real-time experiments to improve transition detection accuracy by addressing system- and user-specific variability through a statistical-based approach and Bayesian optimization.
elements.sent-2411-081: Our findings show that personalized adjustments can remarkably improve the adaptability and effectiveness of lower-limb exoskeletons, paving the way for more responsive and user-centered assistive technologies.
elements.sent-2411-082: The major contributions of this study are the following:.
elements.sent-2411-083: This study rigorously evaluates the ML-trained threshold-based algorithm with two distinct lower-limb exoskeletons, eWalk and autonomyo.
elements.sent-2411-084: The results demonstrate the algorithm's versatility and its ability to adapt to different lower-limb exoskeletons with varied design constraints, validating its broader applicability.
elements.sent-2411-085: We introduce a statistics-based approach and Bayesian optimization to personalize FSM thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-086: We show through experimental validation that these techniques tailored the detection algorithms to individual gait patterns and system-specific characteristics, enhancing the reliability of transition detection.
elements.sent-2411-087: Additionally, we developed a method to address human-exoskeleton joint alignment issues.

elements.sent-2411-115: In this study, we used two distinct lower-limb exoskeletons, eWalk and autonomyo, both developed at EPFL REHAssist research group.
elements.sent-2411-116: The exoskeleton eWalk (eWalk v2) is designed to provide partial assistance for hip flexion/extension (flex./ext.) movement in collaboration with the company Sonceboz \cite{Messara2023, Manzoori2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-117: The lower-limb exoskeleton autonomyo is developed in collaboration with Faulhaber Drive Systems SA to partially assist people with walking impairments due to neuromuscular deficit \cite{Ortlieb2017, orhan2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-118: The autonomyo exoskeleton (autonomyo v2) has 3 active and 3 passive degrees of freedom per leg.
elements.sent-2411-119: The 3 actives are on the hip (abd./add.
elements.sent-2411-120: The remaining passives are at the ankle joint (eversion/inversion, dorsiflexion/plantar flexion, and abd./add.).
elements.sent-2411-121: For hip and knee flexion/extension actuation, each unit consists of a brushless motor and a corresponding gearbox with a 108:1 transmission ratio.
elements.sent-2411-122: An additional cable transmission (2.6:1) is utilized for hip and knee flex./ext.
elements.sent-2411-123: There are integrated torque sensors on the actuator side for the hip and knee flex./ext..
elements.sent-2411-124: Joints are measured on the motor side for both devices.
elements.sent-2411-125: The zero position of the joints is initialized by the homing technique, and with the absolute encoders on the joints for eWalk and autonomyo, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-126: The torso angle with respect to the gravity vector is measured with an IMU (6-axis MPU6050) located on the back module of autonomyo.
elements.sent-2411-127: Ground reaction forces (GRFs) are measured by instrumented soles in both of the devices where on eWalk force sensitive resistor (FSR) based insole is used and eight load cells per foot is utilized for autonomyo.
elements.sent-2411-128: Both exoskeletons are equipped with an embedded computer (BeagleBone Black, Texas Instruments, USA) that collects and keeps the log of all the sensory data on the embedded computer.
elements.sent-2411-129: Wireless communication with both devices is established through a Wi-Fi module.
elements.sent-2411-130: In real-world exoskeleton applications, discrepancies in joint angle measurements often arise due to kinematic constraints and misalignments, such as rotational offsets and center of rotation shifts, with restrictive systems like autonomyo showing greater deviations than more flexible designs like eWalk.
elements.sent-2411-131: Previous work addressed these issues through ergonomic and self-aligning mechanisms \cite{Schiele2006, Stienen2009, Celebi2013, Gálvez2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-132: Instead of focusing on hardware improvements, we propose a versatile mapping algorithm inspired by machine learning regression techniques \cite{Bishop2006 to correct misalignments and improve data accuracy across different exoskeleton systems.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Our focus is on the hip flex./ext.
elements.sent-2411-134: Here, $\pmb{w}$ represents the weights, $\pmb{\phi}$ is a vector of functions of the input $x$, and $t$ is the corrected angle.
elements.sent-2411-135: We aim at setting the free parameters $\pmb{w}$ such that the difference between the function's output $f$ and the desired output $t$ is minimized.
elements.sent-2411-136: To achieve this, we define an optimization problem using a sum of squares loss function $L$ measuring the squared difference between $f$ and $t$ pairs in the dataset: \begin{equation}\begin{split} L(\pmb{w}) = \left( \pmb{t} - f(\pmb{w}, \pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x}))\right)^T\left( \pmb{t} - f(\pmb{w}, \pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x}))\right) = \left( \pmb{t} - \pmb{\phi}(x) \: \pmb{w} \right)^T\left( \pmb{t} - \pmb{\phi}(x) \: \pmb{w} \right) \end{split}\end{equation} And we minimize it with respect to the free parameters $\pmb{w}$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:3} \nabla_{\pmb{w}} L(\pmb{w}) = 0 \longrightarrow \pmb{w}_{opt} = \left[\pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x})^T\pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x})\right]^{-1}\pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x})^T\pmb{t}\end{equation} The final function takes into account position, velocity, and acceleration: \begin{equation}f(x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}) \rightarrow t, \quad f: \mathbb{R}^3 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\end{equation} where $\pmb{w = [w_0, w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4, w_5, w_6]^T$ are the trainable parameters coupled with the linear and nonlinear combinations of position, velocity, and acceleration.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Specifically: \begin{equation}\pmb{\phi}(x) = [1, x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}, x \cdot \dot{x}, x \cdot \ddot{x}, \dot{x} \cdot \ddot{x}]\end{equation} At each time step, we combine the encoder's measured hip position ($x$), velocity ($\dot{x}$), and acceleration ($\ddot{x}$) to produce a corrected hip angle ($t$).
elements.sent-2411-138: As we only have 7 trainable parameters ($\pmb{w}$) to avoid underfitting we train the model on 12 walk-to-stair ascend (W$\rightarrow$SA) transition cycles from 10 subjects, considered ad the most representative of the process, and using literature means as ground truth (GT).
elements.sent-2411-139: We evaluate the training performance by assessing generalizability across subjects, visually confirming the mapping, and calculating the root mean squared error (RMSE) on the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-140: The effectiveness of this formulation comes from the flexibility of the functions $\pmb{\phi}$, which can be chosen as arbitrary nonlinear functions of the input $x$ and its derivatives to handle data nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-141: The weights $w_i$ are optimized to fit specific datasets or processes.
elements.sent-2411-142: In the end, this method offers a flexible, system-agnostic solution for improving exoskeleton data accuracy.
elements.subsection-2411-011: User Specific or System Specific Optimization Methods
elements.sent-2411-143: The trade-off between computational efficiency and generalization capability led to the use of the proposed FSM for transition detection.
elements.sent-2411-144: However, due to this trade-off, the transition detection accuracy may decrease when the FSM encounters gait data from subjects who differ considerably from the training set, especially when using various locomotion support systems tailored for different populations.
elements.sent-2411-145: To address these challenges, the following sections introduce fine-tuning techniques aimed at enhancing detection accuracy, particularly for subjects who represent outlier cases.
elements.sent-2411-146: The Finite-State Machine uses thresholds as upper and lower bounds to detect transitions.
elements.sent-2411-147: For example, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:UpperLowerBounded , the ICF associated during SA$\rightarrow$W and W$\rightarrow$SA transitions must exceed or fall below trained thresholds ($th_{W-SA}$ and $th_{SA-W}$) for detection, in this sense, they are defined as lower and upper bounded respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-148: This approach applies to all transition pairs.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{upper_lower_bounded_v2.pdf} \caption{Visualisation of the upper and lower bounded transitions.} \label{fig:UpperLowerBounded}
elements.sent-2411-149: Fig.~\ref{fig:UpperLowerBounded -B illustrates a scenario where the threshold, tuned on the training population, is too high to detect transitions in the new population, causing the FSM with initially trained thresholds underperform.}
elements.sent-2411-150: To improve transtion detection performance, we propose lowering the thresholds based on the new population's statistical properties, as indicated by the arrow in the figure.
elements.sent-2411-151: This adjustment scales the training thresholds to better fit the new data, as defined by the following formula: \begin{equation}\label{eq:statistics_based} TH_{new} = TH_{tr} \cdot \frac{mean(ICF_i^{new})\pm std(ICF_i^{new})}{mean(ICF_i^{tr})\pm std(ICF_i^{tr})}\end{equation} Where $TH_{tr}$ represents the old threshold from offline training, and $TH_{new}$ is the adjusted threshold after statistics-based tuning.
elements.sent-2411-152: A $+$ sign is used for lower bound transition cases, while a $-$ sign is used otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-153: Summing up, we extract $ICF_i$ for both populations and use Eq.~\ref{eq:statistics_based to adjust the threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-154: We use Bayesian Optimization (BO) to fine-tune these thresholds for specific data when the impact of threshold changes on final performances is unclear and the explicit form of $J(\pmb{TH )$ is unavailable \cite{Garnett_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-155: BO iteratively samples the parameter-output relationship and uses an acquisition function to guide optimization with minimal experiments.
elements.sent-2411-156: To estimate the objective function, we use Gaussian Processes with a Radial Basis Function kernel, implemented via the GPy library in Python \cite{GPy .}
elements.sent-2411-157: This RBF kernel is selected for its smoothness, higher-order derivatives, as well as the capability to provide both mean and uncertainty values as a consequence of data fitting \cite{Rasmussen2006Gaussian .}
elements.sent-2411-158: The GP fitting produces a surrogate function $S(\pmb{TH )$ in terms of mean and variance, which we use to formulate the acquisition function as follows: \begin{equation}A(\pmb{TH}) = k*mean(S(\pmb{TH})) \pm (1-k)*std(S(\pmb{TH}))\end{equation} The parameter $k$ in BO balances exploration and exploitation.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Since our search space includes multiple local minima, we set $k=0.5$ to prioritize balanced exploration and rapid convergence to the global minimum.
elements.sent-2411-160: The acquisition function $A$, derived from a surrogate function, estimates the objective function and reflects the relationship between parameters and performance.
elements.sent-2411-161: Therefore, BO iteratively solves two optimization problems simultaneously: it refines this differentiable surrogate function with new and past data and minimizes the acquisition function to find optimal thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-162: As a result, instead of solving Eq.~\ref{eq:ThresholdsOptimization , the process recursively solves the following: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \pmb{TH}^* = \:\:\: \argmin_{\pmb{TH} \in \mathbb{R}^n} \:\:\: A(\pmb{TH}) \text{subject to} \:\:\:\:\: \pmb{TH} \in \Omega \end{aligned}\end{equation} Algorithm \ref{alg:pseudocode1 provides a pseudocode of the BO process.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: We optimize thresholds in pairs, and the searching space $\Omega$ per each one is set $TH_{W-S/S-W}\:\: \in \:\: [-60,60]$, $TH_{W-SA/SA-W \:\: \in \:\: [30,65]$, $TH_{W-SD/SD-W}\:\: \in \:\: [0,25]$).}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Bayesian Optimization Process}\label{alg:pseudocode1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Variables: $J$, $\pmb{TH}$ \While{not convergence condition} \State $J,\pmb{TH} \gets new\_sample$ \Comment{from experiment/simulation} \State estimate $J(\pmb{TH})$ \Comment{By fitting $\{J,\pmb{TH}\}$ pairs with GP} \State formulate $A(\pmb{TH})$ \State suggest $\pmb{TH}_{\text{next}}$ from $\pmb{TH}_{\text{next}} = \arg \min_{\pmb{TH}} A(\pmb{TH})$ \EndWhile \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-164: The objective function $J(TH_{transit1 , TH_{transit2 )=J(\pmb{TH )$, is evaluated using the following pseudocode given in Algorithm~\ref{alg:BO_objectivefuntion .}}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Objective function evaluation} \label{alg:BO_objectivefuntion} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \If{$\text{GT} = class1$ and $\text{FSM\_output} \neq class1$} \State $J_{j+1}(\pmb{TH}) \gets J_j(\pmb{TH}) + C_1$ \EndIf \If{$transition\_to\_class1\_happened\_before$} \If{$\text{GT} = class$ and $\text{Output\_FSM} \neq class$} \State $J_{j+1}(\pmb{TH}) \gets J_j(\pmb{TH}) + C_2$ \EndIf \EndIf \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-165: In this context, $class1$ refers to the classes the algorithm can detect from ``Walking'' (e.g., Sit, Stair Ascend, Stair Descend), while $class$ always represents Walk.
elements.sent-2411-166: The penalty constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ are heuristically set to $0.005$ and $0.001$, respectively, with $C_1$ being higher to reduce the risk of getting stuck in a local minimum where the system prioritizes the recognition of the second transition.
elements.sent-2411-167: By assigning a higher penalty to the first transition in a sequence, we ensure it is recognized first.
elements.sent-2411-168: Thus, the FSM is penalized whenever it outputs a class different from the ground truth, with greater penalties the longer the time the FSM takes to detect the transition.
elements.sent-2411-169: Furthermore, additional penalties are applied during W$\rightarrow$SA/SA$\rightarrow$W and W$\rightarrow$SD/SD$\rightarrow$W optimizations if threshold values are excessively high or low as described in Algorithm \ref{alg:penalty1 and Algorithm \ref{alg:penalty2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-170: Where, $\pmb{TH = \left[TH_{\text{transit1 , TH_{\text{transit2 }\right]$, and $\alpha$ is a parameter governing the strength of the regularization, with no regularization when $\alpha = 0$ \cite{Goodfellow2016 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-171: In our case, we heuristically set $\alpha=2*10^{-5}$.
elements.sent-2411-172: This regularization term, inspired by neural network weight decay strategies \cite{Chollet2021 , helps prevent getting stuck in local minima while expanding the search space for optimal solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-173: For instance, in W$\rightarrow$SA/SA$\rightarrow$W optimization, setting a too high threshold for W$\rightarrow$SA transition detection causes the algorithm to always classify as ``Walking'' and missing the ``Stair Ascending'' transition thus prioritizing one transition over the other and creating a local minimum.
elements.sent-2411-174: By optimizing both transitions simultaneously and introducing this regularization term, we reduce the risk of encountering such local minimum.

elements.sent-2411-179: Evolution of the acquisition function in BO The iterative updates of the acquisition function throughout the BO process are illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:BO_process .}
elements.sent-2411-180: At Iteration~1, the acquisition function begins with a flat initial guess, indicating a lack of information about the objective function's landscape.
elements.sent-2411-181: As the optimization progresses to Iteration~4, the acquisition function starts to explore the search space, revealing the initial contours of potential areas for optimization.
elements.sent-2411-182: By Iteration~13, the function displays more distinct peaks and valleys, reflecting a refined understanding of the objective landscape and identifying promising regions for further exploration.
elements.sent-2411-183: Finally, at Iteration~30, the acquisition function has notably evolved, showing well-defined global and local minima that correspond to optimal thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-184: This progression demonstrates the BO process's balance between exploration and exploitation, as it efficiently searches the parameter space to find the best solutions while continuously refining its model of the objective function.

elements.section-2411-004: Real-Time Experiments with eWalk and autonomyo
elements.sent-2411-191: The experiment tested four locomotion modes—level walking, stair ascent, stair descent, and sitting—along with six transitions between these modes (W $\rightarrow$ SA, SA $\rightarrow$ W, W $\rightarrow$ S, S $\rightarrow$ W, W $\rightarrow$ SD, and SD $\rightarrow$ W) in the environment shown in Fig~\ref{fig:OnlineTestsOutdoorEnvironment .}
elements.sent-2411-192: Subjects completed 15-step stairs and selected their walking speed and sitting position.
elements.sent-2411-193: Each transition was repeated 50 times per exoskeleton, with each subject performing five trials.
elements.sent-2411-194: For the experiments, eligible adults are aged 18-65, height of 160-200 cm, have EU shoe size 38-45, can consent and follow the study, and are in good health for walking without problems.
elements.sent-2411-195: Those with systemic disorders, seeking treatment, psychiatric/cognitive impairments, insufficient language skills, under guardianship, and pregnant/nursing are excluded.
elements.sent-2411-196: During the tests, ten healthy subjects with the age of $25.6 \pm 2.76$ years, a height of $178.4 \pm 8.1$ cm, and a body mass of $71.9 \pm 9.68$ kg were asked to perform the trial with eWalk.
elements.sent-2411-197: For the tests with autonomyo ten healthy subjects with the age of $26.9 \pm 3.42$ years, the height of $178.2 \pm 8.65 cm$, and a body mass of $72.2 \pm 13.44$ kg were asked to participate.
elements.sent-2411-198: During this test, participants were constantly monitored and helped if needed, to avoid any risk of accident.
elements.sent-2411-199: All participants provided written and informed consent, according to the ethical conduct defined by the EPFL Human Research Ethics Committee, which follows the standards set by the declaration of Helsinki and the Oviedo Convention.
elements.sent-2411-200: During the experiments, eWalk is utilized in passive mode due to the transparent nature of the device with low mass and low friction, and passive back-drivability where autonomyo is utilized in the zero-torque mode which enables transparency without adding additional assistance.

elements.subsection-2411-018: Public Dataset of the Joint Trajectories
elements.sent-2411-261: The joint trajectories captured during the experiments highlight the potential of both the eWalk and autonomyo exoskeletons in facilitating natural human movement across various locomotion modes.
elements.sent-2411-262: The consistency between the joint trajectories in our experiments and those reported in the literature \cite{Reznick2021 reinforces the reliability of the measurements from exoskeletons.}
elements.sent-2411-263: This alignment is particularly evident in the eWalk, where the hip flex./ext.
elements.sent-2411-264: Such accuracy is crucial for ensuring smooth and intuitive human-robot interaction, which directly impacts user comfort and overall system effectiveness in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-265: Despite the growing research on lower-limb exoskeletons, publicly available datasets in this field remain scarce.
elements.sent-2411-266: Most existing datasets focus on non-exoskeleton-assisted gait \cite{Moore2015,Fukuchi2018,Hu2018,Camargo2021,Reznick2021 , limiting opportunities for comparative studies and the development of standardized benchmarks for exoskeletons.}
elements.sent-2411-267: This dataset makes a significant contribution by offering joint trajectory data from two exoskeletons, eWalk and autonomyo.
elements.sent-2411-268: It provides researchers with a valuable resource to systematically evaluate and compare control strategies, advancing the fields of locomotion mode detection and personalization in assistive robotics.
elements.subsection-2411-019: The Importance of the Joint Alignment
elements.sent-2411-269: Accurate joint trajectory data is essential for improving locomotion mode detection, but joint misalignment between human and exoskeleton joints remains a persistent challenge in exoskeleton development \cite{Naf2019, Siviy2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-270: Misalignment, often caused by simplified kinematic models or improper fitting \cite{BesslerEtten2022 , can reduce control accuracy and lead to unwanted interaction forces, negatively impacting user comfort and safety.}
elements.sent-2411-271: Given the complexity of human joint mechanics, completely eliminating kinematic mismatches is impractical \cite{Naf2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-272: Our approach addresses these issues by minimizing the discrepancy between exoskeleton measurements and actual human movement, improving overall system performance.
elements.sent-2411-273: The learning-based approach presented in this work offers a solution when exoskeleton design cannot be individually adapted, yet reliable kinematic data are still necessary.
elements.sent-2411-274: The re-mapped joint angles presented in Fig~\ref{fig:thighAngleTrajectories demonstrate improved alignment with natural transitions, such as MHF and HS, which are consistent with healthy human biomechanics from the literature \cite{Reznick2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-275: For instance, the W$\rightarrow$SD transition cycles, despite initial differences, exhibited reduced variance and key features such as MHF and HS that better reflect natural human movement patterns.
elements.sent-2411-276: This is particularly important for the FSM, which relies on these characteristic features for accurate transition detection.
elements.sent-2411-277: Overall, the mapping function validates our approach, supporting the hypothesis that it can generalize across different gait cycles while better capturing natural human movement.
elements.sent-2411-278: However, the application of the mapping procedure led to mixed results in transition detection accuracy of autonomyo experiments.

elements.sent-2411-306: Although the optimization of the thresholds of the FSM algorithm using BO led to substantial improvements in locomotion transition recognition across most of the subjects, for some subjects transition detection accuracy is not as improved as the others as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:SubjectSpecificChanges_ewalkandAutonomyo_v2 .}
elements.sent-2411-307: These challenges likely stem from non-optimized aspects of the FSM, such as the MHF detector or inaccuracies in previously detected transitions, as well as unique subject-specific behaviors that were not fully accounted for during optimization.
elements.sent-2411-308: For example, in the eWalk experiments, Subject 5’s SA$\rightarrow$W transitions were inaccurately detected due to the timing of MHF detection.
elements.sent-2411-309: This issue was traced back to the MHF detector design rather than the optimization process itself.
elements.sent-2411-310: Specifically, the MHF detector included a velocity range near zero to identify the maximum joint angle in the gait cycle, as the maximum angle occurs when the velocity is close to zero.
elements.sent-2411-311: By expanding this range, we observed improved accuracy but also identified the detector as the root cause of the misclassifications, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:allShowCases -A.}
elements.sent-2411-312: During Subject 7's experiments with eWalk, the FSM exhibited instability during W$\rightarrow$S and S$\rightarrow$W transitions, prematurely transitioning to SD and preventing the correct detection of the intended transition.
elements.sent-2411-313: This instability could not be resolved by adjusting the W$\rightarrow$S/S$\rightarrow$W thresholds alone, as the root cause was linked to the FSM’s logic rather than the parameters optimized by BO.
elements.sent-2411-314: Similarly, misclassifications for Subject 10 were traced to the Loaded/Unloaded threshold.
elements.sent-2411-315: The low threshold, combined with signal noise from uncommon movements, led to incorrect transitions, such as detecting W$\rightarrow$S instead of the intended W$\rightarrow$SA transition.

elements.sent-2411-318: We define instability as when the FSM keeps on switching between two or more classes in consecutive steps, showing confusion and ``unstable output'' (Fig.~\ref{fig:allShowCases -C/D).}
elements.sent-2411-319: This instability was primarily due to subject-specific behaviors, such as taking small, cautious steps—a behaviour likely due to discomfort or fear when using the exoskeleton.
elements.sent-2411-320: These small steps caused the FSM to misclassify the transitions, often failing to detect the correct transition sequence, which hindered the optimization process.

elements.sent-2411-323: These cases demonstrate that certain behavioral adaptations to the exoskeleton, such as taking small steps or misjudging transitions, cannot be fully mitigated through parameter optimization alone.

elements.sent-2411-325: Given that the exoskeleton is intended for individuals with mobility impairments, ensuring a robust response to individual user behaviors is critical for both effectiveness and safety.
elements.sent-2411-326: To mitigate these risks, the controller for active assistance should be designed to balance responsiveness and stability, aiming to reduce both false positives and false negatives in transition detection.
elements.sent-2411-327: Minimizing false positives is essential to prevent unintentional torques that could disrupt balance while reducing false negatives ensures timely and appropriate assistance during transitions.
elements.sent-2411-328: This suggests the need for a less restrictive, more adaptable design of exoskeletons to accommodate the variability in user interactions with the exoskeleton.

elements.sent-2411-342: This study has some limitations that should be acknowledged.
elements.sent-2411-343: A primary limitation of this study is the absence of active assistance in the exoskeletons used during testing.
elements.sent-2411-344: The exoskeletons did not apply assistive torques, meaning user kinematics were unaffected by externally applied torques.
elements.sent-2411-345: When active assistance is enabled, the dynamics of user movement and the associated transition detection may change, potentially increasing false positives or negatives in transition detection.
elements.sent-2411-346: Future research should investigate how assistive torques affect user movement patterns, with particular attention to tuning the FSM parameters to accommodate the changes in gait transitions brought about by assisted movement.
elements.sent-2411-347: Further limitations include the optimization process, which only focuses on threshold parameters within the FSM.
elements.sent-2411-348: Comprehensive optimization of selected parameters for characteristic moments of FSM could potentially enhance the overall system performance.
elements.sent-2411-349: Additionally, although the sample-efficient method of BO shows promise, we did not implement HIL optimization in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-350: Further refinement will be necessary to enable larger-scale parameter optimization within the FSM in real-time applications.
elements.sent-2411-351: Moreover, the current method relies on measurements from the right leg during transitions between walking modes.
elements.sent-2411-352: This could lead to misclassifications if a participant primarily uses the left leg during transitions.
elements.sent-2411-353: To improve robustness, future work should include measurements from both legs to enhance the device’s versatility and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-354: Additionally, the tests were conducted with subjects who had no prior knowledge of the recognition methodology.
elements.sent-2411-355: However, the explainability of the algorithm makes it easily understandable in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-356: We anticipate even better performance if subjects are trained to interact with the algorithm, and future research will explore this aspect to improve human-machine interaction.
elements.sent-2411-357: Ultimately, we recognize the importance of studying diverse populations to ensure the system’s effectiveness and safety for a broader user base.
elements.sent-2411-358: Therefore, our next study will aim to include participants with varying characteristics and mobility impairments, enabling us to evaluate the system’s performance across different user profiles.
elements.sent-2411-359: This study introduced two methods, the Statistics-Based Approach and Bayesian Optimization, to personalize transition detection thresholds of the utilized FSM for lower-limb exoskeletons.
elements.sent-2411-360: Additionally, we addressed joint misalignment issues commonly encountered in human-exoskeleton interactions.
elements.sent-2411-361: Human subject experiments conducted with two distinct exoskeletons, eWalk, and autonomyo, demonstrated the effectiveness of these approaches in accurately detecting transitions between locomotion modes.
elements.sent-2411-362: Mitigating joint misalignment and optimizing FSM thresholds allowed the system to adapt to individual gait patterns and system-specific characteristics, resulting in reliable performance across a diverse user base.
elements.sent-2411-363: Our results underscore the potential of these methods for practical applications in assistive devices, where subject- and system-specific adaptation is critical for enhancing transition detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-364: To support future research, we also provided an open-source dataset of lower-limb kinematics from 18 subjects in total with two exoskeletons, offering a valuable resource for further development in locomotion mode detection and control strategies.

elements.sent-2411-383: Accurate detection of locomotion transitions, such as walk to sit, to stair ascent, and descent, is crucial to effectively control robotic assistive devices, such as lower-limb exoskeletons, as each locomotion mode requires specific assistance.
elements.sent-2411-384: In this study, we identified key factors influencing transition detection performance, including variations in user behavior, and mechanical design of the exoskeletons.
elements.sent-2411-385: Our experimental results demonstrate that both methods remarkably improve detection accuracy across diverse users, achieving up to an 80\% increase in certain scenarios compared to the non-personalized threshold method.
elements.sent-2411-386: By incorporating subject- and system-specific data into the model, our approach offers a precise and reliable solution for detecting locomotion transitions, catering to individual user needs, and ultimately improving the performance of assistive devices.
elements.sent-2411-387: Effective assistance during locomotion activities such as walking, stair ascent, stair descent, and sitting is essential for enhancing user experience in lower-limb exoskeletons, because of this, accurate real-time transition detection is crucial for developing intelligent systems that provide responsive assistance.
elements.sent-2411-388: While most control strategies are designed for steady-state activities like walking, real-world exoskeleton applications need to adapt to diverse tasks and terrains, and variations in user gait and exoskeleton designs make reliable transition detection challenging, as existing methods often struggle in dynamic environments.

elements.sent-2411-390: In this context, most studies on locomotion mode detection rely on body-mounted sensors but lack real-time integration with assistive devices \cite{Zhou2019, Lu2020_2, Narayan2022, Vu2022, Eken2023, Wang2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-391: While prosthetic systems have achieved high real-time performance for recognizing locomotion mode transitions \cite{Young2014b, Su2019, Haque2024 , transition detection in lower-limb exoskeletons remains less explored \cite{Moreira2022 .}}

elements.sent-2411-401: The proposed ML-trained threshold-based method demonstrated high accuracy with ten subjects across walking, sitting, stair ascent, and stair descent in an outdoor setting as presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:OnlineTestsOutdoorEnvironment .}
elements.sent-2411-402: However, using this method with the autonomyo exoskeleton revealed certain limitations.
elements.sent-2411-403: The more complex design of autonomyo, with three active degrees of freedom, affected user behavior differently than the lightweight eWalk, which has a single active degree of freedom.

elements.sent-2411-406: Inter-subject variability in response to assistive strategies suggests that device parameters beneficial to one individual may hinder another, highlighting the need for personalized control tuning \cite{Quesada2016, Kim2017_2, Slade2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-407: Balancing personalization and generalization remains a core challenge in adaptive control systems for assistive devices.
elements.sent-2411-408: While initial personalization approaches relied on subject-specific training data, their practicality is limited by lengthy evaluation times \cite{Zhang2017, Ding2018, Xu2023 .}

elements.sent-2411-410: Building on prior research, our work leverages data from real-time experiments to improve transition detection accuracy by addressing system- and user-specific variability through a statistics-based approach and Bayesian optimization.

elements.sent-2411-422: We use the FSM \cite{Cheng2021 with four states as Sit, Walk, Stair Ascent, and Stair Descent to address six transition detection scenarios, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:OnlineTestsOutdoorEnvironment .}}

elements.sent-2411-431: In this study, we used two lower-limb exoskeletons: eWalk and autonomyo, developed at EPFL REHAssist.

elements.sent-2411-440: Human-exoskeleton joint angle discrepancies often arise from kinematic constraints and misalignments like rotational offsets and shifts in the center of rotation.
elements.sent-2411-441: While ergonomic and self-aligning mechanisms attempted to address these issues \cite{Schiele2006, Stienen2009, Celebi2013, Gálvez2016 , mechanical design improvements are not always feasible.}
elements.sent-2411-442: Hence, we propose a regression-based mapping algorithm to correct misalignments and improve data accuracy, focusing on the hip flex./ext.
elements.sent-2411-443: We formulate a sum of squares loss function between model output and literature data to optimize $\pmb{w}$: \begin{equation}\begin{split} L(\pmb{w}) = \left( \pmb{t} - f(\pmb{w}, \pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x}))\right)^T\left( \pmb{t} - f(\pmb{w}, \pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x}))\right) = \left( \pmb{t} - \pmb{\phi}(x) \: \pmb{w} \right)^T\left( \pmb{t} - \pmb{\phi}(x) \: \pmb{w} \right) \end{split}\end{equation} And we minimize it with respect to the free parameters $\pmb{w}$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:3} \nabla_{\pmb{w}} L(\pmb{w}) = 0 \longrightarrow \pmb{w}_{opt} = \left[\pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x})^T\pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x})\right]^{-1}\pmb{\phi}(\pmb{x})^T\pmb{t}\end{equation} The final function accounts for position and its first and second derivatives as inputs: \begin{equation}f(x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}) \rightarrow t, \quad f: \mathbb{R}^3 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}\end{equation} where $\pmb{w = [w_0, w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4, w_5, w_6]^T$ are the trainable parameters coupled with the linear and nonlinear combinations of position, velocity, and acceleration.}
elements.sent-2411-444: Specifically: \begin{equation}\pmb{\phi}(x) = [1, x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}, x \cdot \dot{x}, x \cdot \ddot{x}, \dot{x} \cdot \ddot{x}]\end{equation} At each time step, we combine the encoder's hip position ($x$), velocity ($\dot{x}$), and acceleration ($\ddot{x}$) to produce a corrected hip angle ($t$).
elements.sent-2411-445: The model, with 7 trainable parameters ($\pmb{w}$), is trained on 12 W-SA transition cycles from 10 subjects, using literature means as ground truth (GT).
elements.sent-2411-446: We evaluate training performance with root mean squared error (RMSE), generalizability across subjects, and visual mapping confirmation.
elements.sent-2411-447: We speculate that the nonlinear flexibility of $\pmb{\phi}$ enables the model to handle data nonlinearity, providing a system-agnostic solution.
elements.sent-2411-448: The proposed ML-trained TH-method balances computational efficiency and generalization for transition detection but accuracy may decrease when handling gait data from subjects significantly different from the training population.
elements.sent-2411-449: To mitigate this, the following sections present fine-tuning techniques to improve detection accuracy, particularly for outlier subjects.
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{upper_lower_bounded_v2.pdf} \caption{Visualisation of the upper and lower bounded transitions.} \label{fig:UpperLowerBounded}
elements.sent-2411-450: We developed the Statistics-Based approach (SBA) to adapt thresholds by analyzing statistical differences between training and subject-specific data.
elements.sent-2411-451: The FSM uses these thresholds as upper and lower bounds to detect transitions, such as in SA-W and W-SA, where the ICF must exceed or fall below trained thresholds ($th_{W-SA}$ and $th_{SA-W}$) for detection, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:UpperLowerBounded -A, and this approach applies to all transition pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-452: Fig.~\ref{fig:UpperLowerBounded -B shows a scenario where the trained threshold is too high for the new gait pattern, causing missed detections.}
elements.sent-2411-453: To improve performance, we propose lowering the thresholds based on the new population's statistical properties, adjusting the training thresholds as defined by the following formula: \begin{equation}\label{eq:statistics_based} TH_{new} = TH_{tr} \cdot \frac{mean(ICF_i^{new})\pm std(ICF_i^{new})}{mean(ICF_i^{tr})\pm std(ICF_i^{tr})}\end{equation} Where $TH_{tr}$ is the old threshold from training, and $TH_{new}$ is the adjusted threshold after tuning.
elements.sent-2411-454: A $+$ sign is used for lower bound transitions, and a $-$ sign otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-455: Summing up, we extract $ICF_i$ for both populations and use Eq.~\ref{eq:statistics_based to scale the threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-456: Although SBA allows us to scale transition thresholds so subject-specific statistics, formally the thresholds can be adjusted by solving an optimization problem: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ThresholdsOptimization} \begin{aligned} \pmb{TH}^* = \:\:\: \argmin_{\pmb{TH} \in \mathbb{R}^n} \:\:\: J(\pmb{TH}) \text{subject to} \:\:\:\:\: \pmb{TH} \in \Omega \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, $J$ is some objective function measuring FSM performance, and $\pmb{TH}$ denotes the optimization thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-457: We use Bayesian Optimization (BO) to fine-tune these thresholds when the impact of threshold changes on performance is unclear and the explicit formulation of $J(\pmb{TH )$ is unavailable \cite{Garnett_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-458: For objective function estimation, we use Gaussian Processes with a Radial Basis Function kernel via the GPy library in Python \cite{GPy , chosen for its smoothness, higher-order derivatives, as well as the capability to provide both mean and uncertainty values from data fitting \cite{Rasmussen2006Gaussian .}}
elements.sent-2411-459: From the GP fitting we derive a surrogate function $S(\pmb{TH )$, which we use to formulate the acquisition function as follows: \begin{equation}A(\pmb{TH}) = k*mean(S(\pmb{TH})) \pm (1-k)*std(S(\pmb{TH}))\end{equation} The parameter $k$ in BO balances exploration and exploitation.}
elements.sent-2411-460: Since our search space has multiple local minima, we set $k=0.5$ to prioritize balanced exploration and rapid convergence.
elements.sent-2411-461: The acquisition function $A$represents an estimate of the objective function and reflects the relationship between parameters and performance.
elements.sent-2411-462: BO iteratively refines the surrogate function with new and past data and minimizes the acquisition function to find optimal thresholds, recursively solving the following instead of Eq.~\ref{eq:ThresholdsOptimization : \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \pmb{TH}^* = \:\:\: \argmin_{\pmb{TH} \in \mathbb{R}^n} \:\:\: A(\pmb{TH}) \text{subject to} \:\:\:\:\: \pmb{TH} \in \Omega \end{aligned}\end{equation} Algorithm \ref{alg:pseudocode1 provides a pseudocode of the BO process.}}
elements.sent-2411-463: We optimize thresholds in pairs, and the searching space $\Omega$ per each one is set $TH_{WtS/StW}\:\: \in \:\: [-60,60]$, $TH_{WtSA/SAtW \:\: \in \:\: [30,65]$, $TH_{WtSD/SDtW}\:\: \in \:\: [0,25]$).}
elements.sent-2411-464: We formulate the objective function $J(\pmb{TH )$ as detailed in the pseudocode given in Algorithm~\ref{alg:BO_objectivefuntion .}}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Objective function formulation} \label{alg:BO_objectivefuntion} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \If{$\text{GT} = class1$ and $\text{FSM\_output} \neq class1$} \State $J_{j+1}(\pmb{TH}) \gets J_j(\pmb{TH}) + C_1$ \EndIf \If{$transition\_to\_class1\_happened\_before$} \If{$\text{GT} = Walk$ and $\text{Output\_FSM} \neq Walk$} \State $J_{j+1}(\pmb{TH}) \gets J_j(\pmb{TH}) + C_2$ \EndIf \EndIf \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-465: Where $class1$ refers to the classes the algorithm can detect from ``Walking'' (e.g., S, SA, SD).
elements.sent-2411-466: The penalty constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ are heuristically set to $0.005$ and $0.001$ respectively, with $C_1$ being higher to reduce the risk of getting stuck in a local minimum where the system prioritizes the recognition of the second transition.
elements.sent-2411-467: Furthermore, to ensure optimized thresholds remain within realistic bounds, we add additional regularization by adding a penalty term to the objective function $J_j$ when thresholds assume values too far away in the searching space.
elements.sent-2411-468: For W-SA/SA-W transitions, a penalty is applied if any threshold exceeds $55^{\circ}$, while for W-SD/SD-W transitions, the penalty is applied when thresholds fall below $5^{\circ}$.
elements.sent-2411-469: In both cases the penalty is proportional to the squared Euclidean distance from the limit values.
elements.sent-2411-470: The penalty is scaled by a regularization factor $\alpha$: \begin{equation}J_j = J_j + \frac{\alpha}{2}||\pmb{TH} - [\text{limit}, \text{limit}]||^2_2\end{equation} where the limit values are $55^{\circ}$ and $5^{\circ}$ for the respective transitions, $\pmb{TH = \left[TH_{\text{transit1 , TH_{\text{transit2 }\right]$, and $\alpha$ is a parameter governing the strength of the regularization, with no regularization when $\alpha = 0$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-471: In our case, we heuristically set $\alpha=2.10^{-5}$.
elements.sent-2411-472: As detailed in Section \ref{sec:Experiments , our experimental scenario included 5 transitions per subject.}
elements.sent-2411-473: We applied BO to subjects and transitions with poor performance, using 2 transitions for training and 3 for evaluation, and optimizing over 30 iterations.
elements.sent-2411-474: To validate the effective exploration and accurate objective estimation, we conducted grid search in parallel to BO.
elements.sent-2411-475: The discrete Grid Search algorithm (GSA) is used as a baseline method to optimize thresholds due to its simplicity and ease of implementation.
elements.sent-2411-476: GSA evaluates a fixed number of conditions and selects the one with the lowest cost.
elements.sent-2411-477: The design space $\Omega$ is the same of BO: $TH_{W-S/S-W \in [-60,60]$, $TH_{W-SA/SA-W \in [30,65]$, $TH_{W-SD/SD-W \in [0,25]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-478: For W-SA/SA-W and W-SD/SD-W, the space is discretized in 2.5-degree increments, and for W-S/S-W in 10-degree increments.
elements.sent-2411-479: The same cost function is evaluated at each point, providing a baseline for comparison with BO.
elements.sent-2411-480: The experiment focused on the four locomotion modes level walking, stair ascent, stair descent, and sitting and the six transitions between them (W-SA, SA-W, W-S, S-W, W-SD, SD-W) in the environment shown in Fig~\ref{fig:OnlineTestsOutdoorEnvironment .}
elements.sent-2411-481: Subjects completed 15-step stairs and selected their walking speed and sitting position, with each transition repeated 50 times per exoskeleton, given the five trials per subject.
elements.sent-2411-482: Eligible participants were healthy adults aged 18-65, with a height of 160-200 cm and EU shoe size 38-45.
elements.sent-2411-483: Exclusion criteria included systemic disorders, psychiatric impairments, and pregnancy.
elements.sent-2411-484: The eWalk experiment included 10 subjects (age $25.6 \pm 2.76$ years, height $178.4 \pm 8.1$ cm, weight $71.9 \pm 9.68$ kg), and the autonomyo test included 10 subjects (age $26.9 \pm 3.42$ years, height $178.2 \pm 8.65$ cm, weight $72.2 \pm 13.44$ kg).
elements.sent-2411-485: All participants provided written consent, in line with ethical standards set by the EPFL Human Research Ethics Committee and the Helsinki Declaration.
elements.sent-2411-486: During the tests, eWalk and autonomyo operated in zero-torque transparent mode.
elements.sent-2411-487: Ground truth data were labeled in real-time by an observer, noting the transition and steady-state locomotion type.

elements.sent-2411-526: The joint trajectories from the eWalk and autonomyo exoskeletons demonstrate their potential to facilitate natural human movement across various locomotion modes.

elements.sent-2411-528: This accuracy is essential for smooth human-robot interaction, enhancing user comfort and system effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-529: Despite growing research, publicly available datasets for exoskeletons remain scarce, with most focusing on non-exoskeleton-assisted gait \cite{Moore2015,Fukuchi2018,Hu2018,Camargo2021,Reznick2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-530: Our dataset\footnote{\url{https://zenodo.org/records/14076104 addresses this gap by providing joint trajectory data from eWalk and autonomyo, serving as a valuable resource for evaluating and comparing assistive robotics control strategies.}}
elements.sent-2411-531: Accurate joint trajectory data is crucial for locomotion mode detection, but joint misalignment between human and exoskeleton joints remains a challenge \cite{Naf2019, Siviy2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-532: Misalignment, often caused by simplified kinematic models or improper fitting \cite{BesslerEtten2022 , reduces control accuracy and user comfort.}
elements.sent-2411-533: While eliminating kinematic mismatches is often tough \cite{Naf2019 , our approach minimizes discrepancies between exoskeleton measurements and human movement, enhancing system performance.}
elements.sent-2411-534: The learning-based mapping function improves the alignment of joint angles with natural transitions, such as MHF and HS, consistent with healthy biomechanics \cite{Reznick2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-535: For example, W-SD transitions showed reduced variance and better representation of human movement as presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:thighAngleTrajectories , which is critical for FSM-based transition detection.}
elements.sent-2411-536: The mapping also improved median W-SD accuracy by 20\% and maintained SA-W accuracy at 100\%, but slightly decreasing W-S and S-W accuracies by 10\% as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:RecognitionAccuracyTransitions2Exo_wandwoMapping .}
elements.sent-2411-537: Despite some declines, overall accuracy improved for many subjects with reduced variability across locomotion modes, showing the mapping function mitigates joint misalignment, enhancing locomotion detection and control.
elements.sent-2411-538: While challenges remain due to user- and system-specific variability, integrating this method into exoskeletons could provide more precise assistance.
elements.sent-2411-539: Individual physiological characteristics and personalized interaction patterns significantly affect human-machine interaction and exoskeleton performance \cite{Kirkwood2021, Massardi2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-540: As users exhibit distinct gait patterns, a strong model capacity to tackle specific cases is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-541: However, limited data availability and computational constraints often hinder the development of generalizable, efficient deep-learning models.
elements.sent-2411-542: In this context, we showed that lightweight ML-trained TH-methods combined with subject-specific optimization, enhance generalization and improve transition detection accuracy across locomotion modes.
elements.sent-2411-543: Results in Fig.~\ref{fig:MeanAccuracyChanges_ewalk_autonomyo_initialvsSBAvsBO show SBA as an effective initial tuning method for simpler systems like eWalk but less effective for complex systems like autonomyo due to its one-step optimization approach.}
elements.sent-2411-544: In contrast, BO achieves superior performance by iteratively refining parameters, and adapting to diverse devices and user variability.
elements.sent-2411-545: For example, BO improved eWalk transitions for S5 (W-SD and SD-W, from 20\% and 40\% to 100\%) and S2 (W-S and S-W, from 80\% and 60\% to 100\%).
elements.sent-2411-546: Similarly, BO significantly improved autonomyo transitions for S2 and 6 (e.g., W-S from 40\% and 60\% to 100\%).
elements.sent-2411-547: In addition, Fig.~\ref{fig:ThresholdChanges_Baseline_BO highlights post-optimization thresholds diverging from baseline values, indicating that often optimal thresholds are unique to individuals with distinct gait patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-548: These findings emphasize the necessity of personalized strategies and support a two-step optimization approach: SBA for initial simpler tuning, followed by BO for robust, adaptive performance if needed in the case of more complex systems such as autonomyo.

Step changes in the GT signal indicate where the transition is happening.} \label{fig:allShowCases}
elements.sent-2411-549: While BO optimization improved transition detection for most subjects as presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:SubjectSpecificChanges_ewalkandAutonomyo_v2 , some challenges remained due to non-optimized FSM components, subject-specific behaviors, and detector design issues.Concerning eWalk, S5's SA-W misclassifications were linked to the MHF detector design, which used a narrow velocity range near zero to detect the maximum in the hip signal.}
elements.sent-2411-550: Expanding this range improved accuracy but revealed the detector’s limitations, as shown in Fig.\ref{fig:allShowCases -A.}
elements.sent-2411-551: S7 experienced FSM instability during W-S and S-W transitions due to FSM implementation rather than threshold issues, while S10 faced noise-induced misclassifications tied to the Loaded/Unloaded threshold, as outlined in Fig.\ref{fig:allShowCases -B.}
elements.sent-2411-552: In the case of autonomyo, S5, 8, and 9 encountered FSM instability during W-SD and SD-W transitions, often due to cautious, small steps or unique non-standard behaviors, such as high leg lifts before descending stairs in S8, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:allShowCases -C/D, where multiple unstable detections are recognizable.}
elements.sent-2411-553: During S9's W-S and S-W transitions, the system incorrectly detected SA, preventing the FSM from detecting the S-W transition, and highlighting issues beyond parameter optimization.
elements.sent-2411-554: These challenges underscore that threshold optimization alone cannot address certain behavioral adaptations but also highlight the potential for further performance improvements if other parameters are considered while fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-555: Overall, controllers must balance responsiveness and stability, minimizing false positives and negatives to ensure safe and effective support.
elements.sent-2411-556: Ultimately, these findings indicate that a more flexible and adaptable exoskeleton design, combined with user training to interact effectively with the algorithm, is crucial for improving the control system's performance and enhancing both safety and effectiveness in human-machine interaction.
elements.sent-2411-557: While personalization is key to improving exoskeleton performance it should also balance real-time computational demands.
elements.sent-2411-558: ML-trained TH-based method outperforms rule-based classifiers on embedded systems \cite{Orhan2023_2 , while deep learning, though viable on platforms like NVIDIA Jetson Xavier \cite{Vandersteegen2019 , is often constrained by specific hardware requirements (i.e. GPUs) and sampling rates \cite{Lu2020 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-559: In this study, we show that event-based state machines can be a computationally efficient alternative.
elements.sent-2411-560: SBA requires initial tuning but supports automated threshold adjustments, while BO eliminates expert input and shows to be four times faster than GSA as presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:CompTime_BO_GSA_ewalk , achieving high performance at low computational cost, completing optimization in 2 minutes on a PC.}
elements.sent-2411-561: Even with the limited computational power of an embedded system, similar outcomes could be achieved within a few minutes.
elements.sent-2411-562: Despite the promising results, this study also reveals some limitations.
elements.sent-2411-563: A primary one is the absence of active assistance in the exoskeletons during testing, meaning user kinematics were unaffected by assistive torques.
elements.sent-2411-564: Expanding parameter optimization beyond thresholds could enhance performance further, and further research should be conducted in this sense.
elements.sent-2411-565: While BO showed strong results, it was not applied in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-566: The reliance on right-leg measurements risks misclassification for left-leg-dominant users; incorporating data from both legs could improve robustness.
elements.sent-2411-567: Future work should focus on large-scale, real-time parameter optimization and include participants with diverse characteristics and mobility impairments to evaluate performance across broader user profiles.
elements.sent-2411-568: This study proposed two methods, the Statistics-Based Approach and Bayesian Optimization, to personalize ML-trained transition thresholds for lower-limb exoskeletons and addressed joint misalignment in human-exoskeleton interactions.
elements.sent-2411-569: Experiments with two exoskeletons, eWalk, and autonomyo, showed these methods effectively detected locomotion mode transitions by adapting to individual gait patterns and system-specific traits, ensuring reliable performance across users.
elements.sent-2411-570: An open-source dataset of lower-limb kinematics from 18 subjects with two exoskeletons is provided to support further research, and the results highlight the potential of proposed methods for assistive devices, where subject- and system-specific adaptation enhances transition detection.
